nodes.label: Matching algorithm comparison (exact hash, perceptual hash, text fingerprint, ensemble, stamped ID)

provenance.quote: But basically, so these are my different matching algorithms.

nodes.label: Exact hash matching

provenance.quote: So exact hash is just a char hash of the page.

nodes.label: Perceptual hash matching

provenance.quote: Perceptual was a P hash, so basically like a visual-based hash of the page.

nodes.label: Text fingerprint matching

provenance.quote: Text for the print is just like a text comparison. How close is the text on the pages?

nodes.label: Ensemble matching

provenance.quote: And then Ensemble was just like a combination of them trying to get them all to work together and fill in for their weaknesses.

nodes.label: Metadata-stamped page ID approach

provenance.quote: So basically what I did was I applied a metadata right into the PDF using Pico PDF to get stamped a unique ID into the page.

nodes.label: Reuse of matching research for duplicate/blank-page detection in sorting

provenance.quote: And we can use this for duplicate detection, like I said, even for blank detection, stuff like that, without involving an LLM.

nodes.label: Product/marketing bundling limited to sorted record sets

nodes.label: question:0004
Will solicitors still be able to redact once docs reach them?
nodes.text: So, would, like, solicitors and them be able to still redact when they get, by the time the docs get to them, or no?
nodes.answer_summary: Redaction is not currently available in the Apprise viewer (though relatively easy to add and much lighter than the old ShareBrief implementation); it has been in the backlog, and the market is trending toward automated rather than manual solicitor-driven redaction.

nodes.statement: Rowan and Lynn discovered they are a little bit allergic to cats while house-sitting a house with seven cats.
nodes.label: Rowan and Lynn discovered a cat allergy during a seven-cat house-sit

provenance.quote: And it was during that house-sitting that we both discovered that we are a little bit allergic to cats.

nodes.statement: Deon discovered his cat allergy during the cat-sitting weekend outside Cardiff.
nodes.label: Deon discovered his cat allergy during the Cardiff cat-sitting trip

provenance.quote: Yeah, that's when we discovered it.

nodes.statement: Deon loved the cat too much over the weekend and it ended up scratching him quite a bit.

nodes.statement: Rowan built a tool that generates synthetic records/PDFs, then resorts them by deleting pages, inserting new pages, rearranging pages, and splitting pages.
nodes.label: Rowan built a synthetic PDF generator and page-resorting tool

provenance.quote: So basically, I generate PDFs, then I resort them. So I've got another command that does some resorting, which does a bunch of different things.
So it deletes pages, it inserts new pages, it rearranges pages, it splits pages, it splits pages and all of that.

nodes.statement: Rowan built a matching engine that uses different matching styles to try to figure out what happened to each page, and reports how reliable each mechanism is at finding what happened to a page.
nodes.label: Rowan built a multi-method page-matching engine

provenance.quote: And then I've got a matching engine, which uses different styles of matching to try and figure out what happened to each page.
And so the output of that is using the different mechanisms, like how reliable are they in finding what happened to a page?

nodes.statement: Once the matching engine correctly identifies the right page, the Apprise Viewer manages to apply moved annotations/redactions correctly.
nodes.label: Apprise Viewer correctly reapplies annotations once the matching page is known

provenance.quote: And then after that, based on that knowledge, I then have used the Apprise Viewer to, like, move annotations around or move redactions around just to kind of see if that works.
Once you know that you've got the page, the right page, it manages to apply them correctly.

nodes.statement: When ingesting annotations in their native format (XFDF), the product already uses the Apprise engine to apply them to pages.
nodes.label: Apprise engine already applies ingested XFDF annotations to pages

provenance.quote: As a quick note, when we ingest, obviously, the annotations, because they're in that format, the XFDF or whatever, we do, like, use the engine of Apprise to apply them to the pages. So that's correct as well.

nodes.statement: The content-hashing matcher can tell that two near-duplicate pages (identical except a small A/B footer difference) are not the same page, but the perceptual-hash matcher is likely to judge them as the same page and miss the small difference.
nodes.label: Exact-hash catches an A/B footer difference; perceptual hash likely misses it

provenance.quote: the matcher that matches on hashing the content will be able to determine that these are not the same page.
It's been like, oh, these still look like the same page. But then it doesn't pick up little tiny things like that.

nodes.statement: Running mri133 gen-corpus generated 12 documents totalling 108 pages into data/corpus.
nodes.label: mri133 gen-corpus generated 12 documents / 108 pages

provenance.quote: generated 12 documents, 108 pages into data/corpus

nodes.statement: Rowan is running the MRI133 tool end-to-end across all matchers on the resorted document set.
nodes.label: Rowan kicks off the MRI133 full end-to-end matcher run

provenance.quote: So, if I go MRI133, this actually runs it end-to-end and runs each matcher.
This will take a little while.

nodes.statement: Deon has to step away in person to receive a delivery because it cannot just be left for pickup and the courier needs to see his face.
nodes.label: Deon steps away to accept an in-person delivery

provenance.quote: Rowan, can I go get the delivery quick? Sorry. Because they need to see my face. It's not a one that they can pick up.

nodes.label: Stamped ID survived page reinsertion, naturally the strongest identifier
nodes.statement: The metadata stamp survived Rowan's page-insertion/resorting method and was naturally the strongest way of identifying the original page.

provenance.quote: I did that stamp, and that survived at least my method of inserting new pages and whatever. And so that obviously was naturally the strongest way of identifying the original.

nodes.label: Exact hash: 92.9% precision on confident pages
nodes.statement: Exact hash matching got 92.9% of its confident (non-abstained) page-outcome predictions right.

provenance.quote: Of those that it was confident about, it got 92.9% right, which is good, right?
exact_hash      P 92.9% R 89.7% abst-ok 81.2% silent 5.6% / queue 22.2%

nodes.label: Exact hash only works on text-bearing / clean pages
nodes.statement: Exact hash matching only works for pages that have text, or clean-looking pages.

provenance.quote: But that only works for things that maybe have text. Or clean-looking things, yeah.

nodes.label: Exact hash fails badly on split pages
nodes.statement: Exact hash is really bad at detecting a split page: because the two resulting pages don't hash the same, it thinks the original page is simply gone.

provenance.quote: So anyway, it's really bad at split. So actually, if a page has been split, it basically thinks the page is gone because there's nothing that's, those two pages don't hash.

nodes.label: Stamped route is by far the best, removes matching noise
nodes.statement: Going with the stamped-ID route is by far and above the best approach: it gets rid of all the noise involved in trying to figure out the right matching.

provenance.quote: And then, basically, the conclusion is that if we go stamped, like, the stamped route is by far and above, like, it gets rid of all the crazy noise and trying to figure out, like, how to do the right matching.

nodes.label: Stamped approach only errs on true duplicates or unlinked splits
nodes.statement: The stamped approach only gets it wrong when a page has been exactly duplicated without re-stamping, or split without any lineage recorded into the previous page; every other scenario it catches because the metadata stamping is maintained.

provenance.quote: Like, if you do stamped, it works, and the only time it gets it wrong is if you've got a exact, like, a duplicate, like, you literally have duplicated the page, and now it's there twice with the same ID, and you haven't re-stamped. Or you've split a page, and you haven't put any kind of lineage into the previous page. Every other scenario, it obviously catches, because you've maintained the metadata stamping.

nodes.label: None of the matchers are accurate enough for the goal
nodes.statement: None of the matching approaches, to Deon's eye/reasoning, are accurate enough for what they are trying to achieve; it's just not going to work.

provenance.quote: I mean, I like the stamp, but none of these, to my eye or to my, like, sort of reasoning, are accurate enough. If you think about what we're trying to achieve here, it's just not going to work.

nodes.label: Redaction estimate: ~75% of annotations placed correctly, 25% need replacement
nodes.statement: For redactions specifically, the best achievable outcome is roughly three quarters of annotations placed back where they are confidently correct, with about 25% needing to be replaced.

provenance.quote: Like, the best we can possibly get is, like, three quarters of your annotations are placed back to where we're confident they are, and 25% of them you're going to need to replace.

nodes.label: Rowan's work also solves duplicate detection in the sorting algorithm for Sally
nodes.statement: Rowan's matching research incidentally solves a separate existing problem: duplicate detection in the record-sorting algorithm relevant to Sally's work.

provenance.quote: Do you know what you've actually done now is you've solved another problem we have, which is duplicate detection in the sorting algorithm for Sally.

nodes.label: Lara would be very interested, this is fascinating
nodes.statement: Lara would be very interested in this research because it is fascinating.

provenance.quote: And Lara would be very interested in all of this, because this is fascinating.

nodes.label: This is deterministic methodology, no LLM used
nodes.statement: The matching research is all deterministic methodology; no AI/LLM was used.

provenance.quote: Without using an LLM, because you didn't obviously use any AI. This is all deterministic methodology.

nodes.label: Rowan expected better results than achieved
nodes.statement: Rowan thought the matching approaches would perform better than they actually did.

provenance.quote: I thought that it would do better than it did.

nodes.label: Deon had high hopes for hashing but it isn't accurate enough
nodes.statement: Deon had high hopes that solid hashing algorithms would reliably detect similar pages, but it turns out they are just not accurate enough.

provenance.quote: I actually had high hopes for this. I thought it was going to be, like, okay, cool. Like, a solid algorithm and solid hashing or, like, those things would be able to detect the similar pages. But I guess it's just not.

nodes.label: The approach hasn't been tried on real data yet
nodes.statement: The matching approach has not yet been tried on real (non-synthetic) data.

provenance.quote: And we haven't even tried it in real data.

nodes.label: Rowan's synthetic dataset is purposefully challenging
nodes.statement: Rowan's synthetic test dataset is purposefully challenging, with pages that look almost identical except for slight footer differences.

provenance.quote: But, like, my data set is purposefully challenging. You know what I mean? Like, there's pages that look exactly the same. I have, like, just got the footer that's, like, slightly different.

nodes.label: Testing on real data won't go to waste
nodes.statement: Trying the approach as an extension of the research on real data won't go to waste.

provenance.quote: You can try it as an extension of the research because I don't think it will go to waste.

nodes.label: No margin for error given the accuracy issues
nodes.statement: Because of the accuracy issues with the matching approaches, there is no margin for error in what they are working with.

provenance.quote: But my feeling here is obviously, like I said, like, because of the accuracy issue with what we're working with, I don't, there's just no margin for error, really.

nodes.label: Redaction currently unavailable in Apprise viewer
nodes.statement: Redaction is currently not available inside the Apprise viewer; it is relatively easy to implement but has not been prioritised despite being in demand and sitting in the feature backlog.

provenance.quote: Deon: So, redaction is currently not available inside the appraise viewer that we have. It is relatively easy to do, to be honest.
Deon: But we just haven't had time to implement it, even though it's been in demand. But it's been one of those things where it's like, ah, it's in the feature backlog.

nodes.label: New viewer's redaction would be lighter than ShareBrief's
nodes.statement: Redaction in the current viewer would be much lighter (less processing-heavy) than the old ShareBrief viewer's redaction, mostly because technology has advanced.

provenance.quote: Deon: I remember you guys had a viewer for ShareBrief that redaction was quite processing heavy. This one is much lighter. Mostly because of, like, technology advancing, and it's not the same sort of thing anymore.

nodes.label: Market trending toward automated redaction
nodes.statement: The market is gearing towards automated redaction, where the system searches and redacts automatically rather than solicitors manually redacting documents.

provenance.quote: Deon: And also, like, the market is gearing towards automated redaction. So, you want something that you don't manually go and redact. Like, it searches, like, and it redacts for you because solicitors, like, don't really want to go and, like, have to do that.

nodes.label: XOD format incompatible with byte-range requests
nodes.statement: The XOD format used by the Apprise viewer is not compatible with byte-range requests; it only renders the pages currently being viewed, so the whole PDF must be loaded into browser memory to manipulate visible pages.

provenance.quote: Deon: So, with the date range filter, since you're in the appraise world, in that viewer, it's not compatible with the format that we use with the XOD because that uses byte range requests, and it only renders the pages that you're viewing, basically.
Deon: So, we rely heavily for that solution that the entire PDF has been loaded into the memory of the browser so that it can manipulate the pages that are visible.

nodes.label: External party said linearized PDFs support byte-range requests
nodes.statement: The external party who called about the XOD format said the XOD is the old way of doing things, and recommended linearized PDFs to also support byte-range requests.

provenance.quote: Deon: So when we were speaking to you the other day, because they did have a call with us, they said, oh, but the XOD is the old way of doing things, which I totally understand. And they recommend linearized PDFs to also do byte range requests.
Deon: But I think that the byte range request is just not going to be compatible. You're going to have to load the whole thing up front.

nodes.label: Byte-range requests won't be compatible; whole PDF must load upfront
nodes.statement: The byte-range request approach is not going to be compatible for this use case; the whole PDF will need to be loaded upfront.

provenance.quote: Deon: But I think that the byte range request is just not going to be compatible. You're going to have to load the whole thing up front.

nodes.label: Only Computer Electronic Records likely to reach ~1000 pages
nodes.statement: Only the Computer Electronic Records document is likely to reach page counts near a thousand; normal documents don't usually have that many pages.

provenance.quote: Deon: I don't think we'll reach that limit because the Computer Electronic Records is the only one that has that many pages. Normal sort of like documents don't really have that many pages, but I mean, it could be.

nodes.label: Adam (CEO) prefers not to record certain discussions
nodes.statement: Adam, as CEO, is very much in favour of stopping the recording for certain discussions.

provenance.quote: Deon: So Adam has been, Adam as CEO is very heavy on, can we stop recording?

nodes.statement: Bundle the insight/redaction-retention benefits as a marketing proposition, limited to sorted record sets.
nodes.rationale: Insights and redaction-retention benefits both only apply to sorted record sets, so bundling them together strengthens the product marketing angle.

provenance.quote: Much of the insights also only applies to sorted sets of records. So we can bundle it as a marketing thing.

nodes.label: decision:0001

nodes.statement: Test the matching approach on real (non-synthetic) records rather than only the synthetic dataset.

provenance.quote: We can try it. I mean, these pages are all quite simple.

nodes.label: decision:0002
Implement redaction inside DocSorter at the sorting stage
nodes.statement: Redaction should be implemented inside DocSorter at the sorting stage, since clinicians are the people best placed to perform the actual redaction, rather than as a single solution covering everything.
nodes.rationale: Clinicians would be the people who are best placed to do the actual redaction in the first place.
alternatives_considered: A single unified solution attempting to solve everything at once (Chantal's original ticket approach), which was found not to work and needed to be broken up into separate solutions (redaction in DocSorter, annotations attached to a page, etc.).

provenance.quote: Deon: So, redaction, my thinking, I might have mentioned this, is also that we do redaction inside DocSorter because they're the people who, the clinicians would be the people who are best placed to do the actual redaction in the first place.

Deon: And that's why I feel like Chantal's original ticket was trying to find something that will solve everything, but it actually can't. But you do need to break it up into separate solutions. And redaction in DocSorter is, like, one part of that.

nodes.description: Speak to Chantal about limiting the sorted-set-only bundling/insights offering.
nodes.source_quote: But then what I'll speak to Chantal about is, like, this will, like I said, this will only be a thing that we can apply for a sorted set of records.

provenance.quote: But then what I'll speak to Chantal about is, like, this will, like I said, this will only be a thing that we can apply for a sorted set of records.

nodes.label: action:0001

nodes.description: Test the matching approach on real (non-synthetic) records.
nodes.source_quote: We can try it. I mean, these pages are all quite simple. ... And you can try it on the records.

provenance.quote: We can try it.

nodes.label: action:0002

nodes.description: Locate the storage account containing real records for testing.
nodes.source_quote: I'll find the storage account quickly. And you can try it on the records.

provenance.quote: I'll find the storage account quickly.

nodes.label: action:0003
Research date-range filtered PDF rendering at scale in the Apprise viewer
nodes.description: Research how to render a date-range-filtered subset of pages in the Apprise viewer, given the XOD format's incompatibility with byte-range requests, and test rendering performance at scale (e.g. against a PDF with roughly a thousand pages or more, such as the Computer Electronic Records).
nodes.source_quote: Deon: I do have another little bit of research I want you to do if you have time. ... So if you can do a little bit of research for me on that, just to send you to again what I've found, that would be very, very useful.

provenance.quote: Deon: I do have another little bit of research I want you to do if you have time.
Rowan: I mean, I can easily generate some fat PDFs and see what happens, yeah.
Deon: So if you can do a little bit of research for me on that, just to send you to again what I've found, that would be very, very useful.

nodes.label: Personal catch-up: weekend and cat-sitting
nodes.summary: Rowan and Deon catch up on their weekends before the technical discussion; Deon describes a cat-sitting trip near Cardiff and discovering a cat allergy.

nodes.label: Screen share: synthetic PDF test harness walkthrough
nodes.summary: Rowan shares his screen and walks Deon through the synthetic PDF/records generator, the resorting tool (deletes, inserts, rearranges, splits pages), and the page-matching engine that tracks what happened to each page.

nodes.label: Running the end-to-end matcher; delivery interruption
nodes.summary: Rowan kicks off the full end-to-end MRI133 run across all matchers. Deon is interrupted by a delivery requiring his physical presence (his partner Lewis is mentioned) and steps away briefly before returning.

nodes.label: Reviewing matcher results and the stamped-ID approach
nodes.summary: Rowan explains the matching algorithms (exact hash, perceptual hash, text comparison, ensemble) and their precision/recall/silent-error tradeoffs. The metadata-stamped page ID approach is by far the most reliable, failing only on true duplicates or unlinked splits.

nodes.label: Implications: duplicate detection reuse and product bundling
nodes.summary: Deon realises Rowan's work also solves duplicate/blank-page detection for the record-sorting algorithm (relevant to Sally's and Chantal's work), all without an LLM. He proposes bundling insight/redaction guarantees as a marketing angle limited to sorted record sets.

nodes.label: Reflection and proposal to test on real data
nodes.summary: Both reflect on the results being less accurate than hoped despite deterministic hashing methods. They agree to try the approach against real (not synthetic) records, with Deon offering to locate the storage account.

nodes.label: Redaction workflow and DocSorter placement
nodes.summary: Deon explains that redaction should live in DocSorter at the sorting stage, since clinicians are best placed to redact. The current Apprise viewer lacks redaction; the market is trending toward automated redaction rather than manual solicitor workflows.

nodes.label: New research ask: date-range filtering and PDF rendering at scale
nodes.summary: Deon asks Rowan to research how to render a date-range-filtered subset of pages in the Apprise viewer, given the XOD format's incompatibility with byte-range requests, and to test performance against very large PDFs.

nodes.label: Wrap-up: CEO's recording preference
nodes.summary: Deon raises an off-topic point about Adam (CEO) preferring not to record certain discussions, and asks to stop recording, ending the captured portion of the meeting.

nodes.label: Screen share begins: VS Code with the annotations/mri133 PDF-matching project open

nodes.label: Screen share: gen-corpus and resort tool output in terminal

provenance.quote:
Rowan.Reid@MB-CPT-LT-0021 annotations % ./mri133 gen-corpus
generated 12 documents, 108 pages into data/corpus
Rowan.Reid@MB-CPT-LT-0021 annotations % ./mri133 resort
re-sorted 1 document(s) -> data/resorted/  (originals in data/corpus/)

  removed_wrong_patient  9 -> 8  a wrong-patient page is deleted (must be flagged removed, not slid to a neighbour)
  removed_benign         9 -> 8  an ordinary page is removed
  split                  9 -> 10  one page is split into two children (annotation duplicates; redaction abstains)
  clone                  9 -> 11  one page is placed in several locations
  full_repagination       9 -> 9  every page index changes at once
  inserted_indent        9 -> 10  pages inserted with indent numbering between existing pages
  reorder                9 -> 9  pages reordered without removal (a re-sort)
  composed_resort        9 -> 10  a realistic re-sort: remove wrong-patient, split a page, clone a page, repaginate

Open a re-sorted PDF next to its original to see the change, e.g.:
  open data/corpus/matter-000-doc-00.clean.pdf data/resorted/matter-000-doc-00.composed_resort.pdf
Rowan.Reid@MB-CPT-LT-0021 annotations %

nodes.label: Screen share: MRI-133 bake-off results (content arm vs stamped arm)

provenance.quote:
open data/corpus/matter-000-doc-00.clean.pdf data/resorted/matter-000-doc-00.composed_resort.pdf
Rowan.Reid@MB-CPT-LT-0021 annotations % ./mri133 run --matchers all
=====================================================================
MRI-133 matching bake-off -- these two arms answer DIFFERENT questions
reference type: ANNOTATION | 12 docs x 8 §9 scenarios
=====================================================================

CONTENT ARM -- the AS-IS answer for the page-less population (nothing stamped):
  precision/recall reported separately; silent-error and review-queue as a PAIR
  exact_hash      P 92.9% R 89.7% abst-ok 81.2% silent 5.6% / queue 22.2%
    worst: SPLIT 100% silent, REMOVED 33% silent, DUPLICATE 7% silent
  perceptual      P 30.8% R 6.9% abst-ok 23.7% silent 12.5% / queue 81.9%
    worst: INSERTED_INDENT 100% silent, REMOVED 33% silent
  text_fingerprint P 60.0% R 25.9% abst-ok 27.7% silent 13.9% / queue 65.3%
    worst: BLANK 100% silent, SPLIT 100% silent, REMOVED 33% silent
  ensemble        P 70.7% R 60.9% abst-ok 59.1% silent 20.4% / queue 30.6%
    worst: INSERTED_INDENT 100% silent, SPLIT 100% silent, REMOVED 33% silent
  DoD reading (content-arm ensemble): POOR  [bars PROPOSED (pending decision): precision>=0.95, recall>=0.7, queue<=0.3]

STAMPED ARM -- an 'if we stamp at extraction' PROJECTION (not today's population):
  stamped_id      P 77.8% R 96.6% abst-ok N/A silent 22.2% / queue 0.0%
    worst: DUPLICATE 100% silent, SPLIT 100% silent

(run with --by-case for the full per-scenario table)

SYNTHETIC-vs-REAL delta: real pairs N/A (synthetic only).
  ** synthetic is a LOWER BOUND on difficulty; the real-pair delta is deferred Milestone C **
=====================================================================

(machine-readable result written to data/bakeoff.json)
(visual report with every percentage's complement written to data/bakeoff.html)
Rowan.Reid@MB-CPT-LT-0021 annotations %

nodes.description: VS Code with PDF page viewer behind a terminal window showing the output of ./mri133 run --matchers all: a comparison of matching approaches (exact_hash, perceptual, text_fingerprint, ensemble) under a content-only arm versus a stamped_id arm, with precision/recall/abstain/silent/queue percentages per matcher and per-worst-case scenario notes, plus a stated DoD reading of POOR for the content-arm ensemble.

nodes.label: Screen share: bakeoff.html visual results (content arm, exact_hash and perceptual)

provenance.quote:
master split (of all pages) exactly one bucket; these add up to 100%
72.2%   Correct 72.2% (624/864)  Silent error (confident & WRONG) 5.6% (48/864)  Over-abstain (needless queue) 18.1% (156/864)
Recall 89.7% of actionable pages: of pages where a confident answer was wanted, how many did it get right?
89.7%   Found (correct) 89.7% (624/696)  Missed (wrong or needlessly queued) 10.3% (72/696)
Abstention judgement 81.2% of all pages: of the pages it hands to a human vs handles itself
77.8%  Sent to queue 22.2% (192/864)  Right to abstain 81.2% (156/192)  Needless (over-abstain) 18.8% (36/192)

pages whose content did not change and simply moved: ordinary, reordered, and inserted pages, and even form letters (their fingerprints differ).
splitting: a cut page's halves are physically new content, so it wrongly declares the page gone. It also cannot confirm a clone, so it safely queues it.
content is untouched, blind the moment content is physically altered.

CONTENT ARM   n = 864 page-outcomes
print of what the page looks like, not its exact bytes. A small visual change (scanner noise, a slight skew) barely moves the fingerprint, how visually alike two pages are.

6.9%   Found (correct) 6.9% (48/696) ... 93.1%

nodes.description: Browser-rendered HTML report (bakeoff.html) with bar charts breaking down precision, recall, and abstention judgement for the exact_hash and perceptual matchers within the content arm, plus explanatory notes on why perceptual hashing fails on split pages (content is physically altered) and succeeds on moved/reordered pages (fingerprint unchanged).
